questions.stem: Which group of organisms are prokaryotes that lack a true membrane-bound nucleus?
questions.explanation: Bacteria are prokaryotes, meaning their cells have no membrane-bound nucleus and their genetic material lies free in the cytoplasm. Fungi, protozoa, and algae are eukaryotes, which do have a nucleus enclosed by a membrane.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: Which statement best describes a virus?
options: It is a free-living single-celled organism
It is a type of fungus
It is an infectious particle that can only reproduce inside a host cell
It always has a nucleus and mitochondria
questions.explanation: A virus is a small infectious particle made of genetic material inside a protein coat. It is not a complete cell and can only reproduce by taking over the machinery of a living host cell. It is not free-living, is not a fungus, and has no nucleus or organelles.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: The rigid bacterial cell wall is made mainly of which substance?
questions.explanation: Bacterial cell walls are built mainly of peptidoglycan, a mesh of sugars and short peptides that gives the cell shape and protection. Cellulose forms plant cell walls, chitin forms fungal cell walls, and cholesterol is a membrane lipid in animal cells.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: The Gram staining technique is used to divide bacteria into two main groups based on differences in what?
questions.explanation: Gram staining separates bacteria into Gram positive and Gram negative groups according to differences in the structure and thickness of their cell walls, which affects how they retain the stain. It does not directly read DNA sequence, flagella speed, or temperature preference.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: Gram positive bacteria appear which color after a correctly performed Gram stain?
questions.explanation: Gram positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan layer that retains the crystal violet dye, so they appear purple or violet. Gram negative bacteria lose the violet dye and take up the counterstain, appearing pink or red.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: Bacteria most commonly reproduce by which process?
questions.explanation: Most bacteria reproduce asexually by binary fission, in which one cell copies its DNA and splits into two identical daughter cells. Mitosis and meiosis are forms of eukaryotic cell division, and budding from a parent animal is not how bacteria divide.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: A spherical bacterium is described by which shape term?
questions.explanation: A coccus is a spherical or round bacterium. A bacillus is rod shaped, a spirillum is rigid and spiral shaped, and a vibrio is curved or comma shaped. These shape terms are a basic way of classifying bacteria.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: A rod-shaped bacterium is described by which shape term?
questions.explanation: A bacillus is a rod-shaped bacterium. A coccus is spherical, a spirochete is a long flexible spiral, and a diplococcus is a pair of spherical cells. The basic bacterial shapes are spheres, rods, and spirals.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: Which structure allows many bacteria to move through their surroundings?
questions.explanation: A flagellum is a whip-like appendage that rotates to propel a bacterium through liquid. Ribosomes make proteins, bacteria have no true nucleus, and chloroplasts are found in plant and algal cells, not in bacteria.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: Some bacteria form highly resistant, dormant structures that survive harsh conditions such as heat and drying. What are these structures called?
questions.explanation: Certain bacteria produce endospores, tough dormant structures that can survive extreme heat, drying, and chemicals, then grow into active cells when conditions improve. Flagella are for movement, pili help attachment and transfer, and vacuoles are storage spaces.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: What does the genetic material (genome) of a virus consist of?
options: Always both DNA and RNA together
questions.explanation: A virus carries its genetic information as either DNA or RNA, but not both at once. The genome is enclosed in a protein coat called a capsid. Proteins and carbohydrates may be present in the virus, but they are not the genetic material.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: The protein coat that surrounds and protects the genetic material of a virus is called what?
questions.explanation: The capsid is the protein coat that encloses and protects a virus's nucleic acid. A cell wall surrounds many cells but viruses are not cells, a plasmid is a small DNA ring in bacteria, and cytoplasm is the internal fluid of a cell.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: Which of the following diseases is caused by a virus?
questions.explanation: Influenza is caused by the influenza virus. Tuberculosis, cholera, and tetanus are caused by bacteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Vibrio cholerae, and Clostridium tetani), so they are bacterial rather than viral diseases.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: Which of the following diseases is caused by a bacterium?
questions.explanation: Tuberculosis is caused by the bacterium Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Measles, influenza, and the common cold are caused by viruses, so antibiotics that target bacteria would not cure them.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: Fungal cell walls are made mainly of which substance?
questions.explanation: Fungal cell walls are built mainly of chitin, a tough carbohydrate also found in insect exoskeletons. Peptidoglycan forms bacterial walls and cellulose forms plant walls, while keratin is a structural protein in animal hair and nails.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: Yeasts, which are single-celled fungi, often reproduce asexually by which process?
options: Binary fission into two equal cells
questions.explanation: Yeasts commonly reproduce asexually by budding, in which a small outgrowth forms on the parent cell, enlarges, and eventually separates as a new cell. They do not photosynthesize, and endospore formation is a bacterial survival process, not yeast reproduction.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: How do fungi obtain their nutrients?
options: By fixing nitrogen from the air
By absorbing nutrients from organic matter, often as decomposers
questions.explanation: Fungi are heterotrophs that feed by secreting enzymes onto organic material and absorbing the digested nutrients. Many act as decomposers, breaking down dead matter. They lack chlorophyll, so they cannot photosynthesize.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: Antibiotics such as penicillin are generally effective against which kind of pathogen?
options: All living and non-living infectious agents equally
questions.explanation: Antibiotics target features of bacteria, such as their cell walls or protein-making machinery, so they treat bacterial infections. They do not work against viruses, which lack those structures, and antibiotics is not the correct term for the medicines used against fungi.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: During the lag phase of a bacterial growth curve, what is mainly happening?
options: Cells adjust to their environment with little increase in number
Cells divide as fast as possible
The population stays constant because births balance deaths
Cells die faster than they are produced
questions.explanation: In the lag phase, newly introduced bacteria adjust to their environment and make enzymes and other materials needed for growth, so cell numbers rise little. Rapid division happens later in the log (exponential) phase, balance occurs in the stationary phase, and net dieoff is the death phase.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: During which phase of bacterial growth do cells divide most rapidly at a roughly constant rate?
questions.explanation: In the log or exponential phase, nutrients are plentiful and bacteria divide at their maximum and roughly constant rate, so the population grows rapidly. The lag phase precedes it, the stationary phase follows when growth and death balance, and the death phase is net decline.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: A microorganism that requires oxygen to grow is described as what?
questions.explanation: An obligate aerobe needs oxygen for its metabolism and cannot grow without it. An obligate anaerobe is harmed by oxygen, a virus is not a free-living organism, and a spore is a dormant survival structure, so none of those describes an oxygen-requiring microbe.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: The process of completely destroying or removing all microorganisms, including resistant endospores, from an object is called what?
questions.explanation: Sterilization is the process that destroys or removes all forms of microbial life, including tough endospores, often using high heat such as an autoclave. Fermentation is an energy-releasing metabolic process, photosynthesis makes food from light, and inoculation introduces microbes into a medium.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: Some bacteria carry small, circular pieces of DNA that are separate from the main chromosome. What are these called?
questions.explanation: Plasmids are small circular DNA molecules in many bacteria that replicate independently of the main chromosome and often carry extra genes, such as those for antibiotic resistance. Ribosomes make proteins, capsids enclose viruses, and endospores are dormant survival structures.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text

questions.stem: Which statement correctly compares the typical size of viruses and bacteria?
options: Both viruses and bacteria are easily seen with the naked eye
Viruses are generally much smaller than bacteria
Viruses are generally much larger than bacteria
Viruses and bacteria are always the same size
questions.explanation: Viruses are generally much smaller than bacteria; most viruses are too small to be seen even with an ordinary light microscope, while many bacteria can be seen with one. Neither is visible to the naked eye.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory microbiology text
